EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds occurred during the afternoon of April 9th with a measured gust to 58 mph at Gary Airport.

Remnant circulation from tropical storm Cristobal combined with strong, negatively tilted phasing shortwave disturbance moved across the area providing strong synoptic winds ahead of the storms. Strong, dynamic cold front initiated storms just after the noon hour with damaging winds, hail, and heavy rain observed during the afternoon. Strong post frontal drying within fierce mid level dry intrusion yielded 40 to 60 mph wind gusts and blowing dust late afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter estimated 60 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system brought periods of mixed precipitation to central Indiana late during the evening of the 17th and continuing through the night.  Precipitation began as a snow/sleet mix and transitioned to freezing rain before all rain. Freezing rain caused slick spots across surfaces and led to several accidents with one being fatal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fatal accident occurred around 9:30 PM local time when an SUV lost control of his vehicle on a bridge and crashed into a nearby pond. There were two occupants of the vehicle.  One occupant was pronounced dead and the other was in critical condition.  A mixture of freezing rain and sleet was falling at the time and police have determined that weather likely played a factor in the crash.

Strong north winds gusting as high as 50 mph produced waves as high as 18 feet along the Lake Michigan shore of northwest Indiana.  These high waves combined with high lake levels produced shoreline flooding, erosion and damage.  In Lake County, Lakefront Park in Whiting, Indiana was completely inundated.  In Porter County, a sea wall that protects homes collapsed in Beverly Shores.  Lakefront Drive was closed from Broadway to Dunbar due to large pieces of debris and ice chunks being deposited onto the road by the waves.  Severe erosion at Lake View Beach also forced the closure of the section of Lakefront Drive nearest the beach, east of Broadway.  Large ice chunks were deposited by the waves onto Lakefront Drive on the east side of Beverly Shores.

On March 28th, a strong low pressure system brought 3 rounds  of severe thunderstorms to central Indiana.  The first round brought widespread 1 to 1.75 inch hail to much of the area with the second event bringing additional large hail and flash flooding.  A third round during the late evening and overnight brought additional heavy rain and damaging wind gusts.  A microburst caused an intense area of significant damage to portions of Morgan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst brought significant damage to portions of Mooresville.  Several small trees had tree trunks snapped.  Roof damage was common with widespread tree limbs snapped. Damage was fairly isolated but significant. Video of the event showed very intense winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On March 19th and 20th, two cold fronts had an affect on the region. The front on the 19th caused heavy rain to drop into southern Indiana. The front on the 20th was a long front that stretched from the Great Lakes straight to the middle of Texas. As this front moved towards the southeast, it caused a line of severe winds and localized flooding over much of the southeastern United States.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and branches were reported down in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the aftermath of a frontal passage during the overnight hours of March 28th, strong gradient winds brought gusty winds to central and north central Indiana during the daytime of March 29th.  These gusty winds led to isolated areas of wind damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous rain events led to renewed river flooding in March. Minor flooding was widespread across the region, including the Ohio, Wabash, White, and Patoka Rivers. The impact of  the flooding was mainly low-lying woods and fields in bottomland areas. Some rural county roads near the rivers were flooded. Along the White and Wabash Rivers, oil fields near the rivers were inaccessible. Some cabins near those rivers were also inaccessible. Monthly rainfall was 2 to 4 inches above normal for most of the area. Wet soil conditions contributed to runoff occurring fairly quickly with heavy rain. The flooding continued into April on most of the rivers.

A warm front was located across central Indiana and Ohio during the early morning hours of the 28th. An influx of moisture combined with marginal shear profiles and moderate instability all lead to several thunderstorms forming across portions of northwest Ohio. The elevated nature of these storms kept them confined to hail production, which was quite limited across this area.

A brief snow event on March 14th brought slick road conditions to central Indiana.  During the event, a fatal car accident occurred in Shelby County with generally fair road conditions across much of the rest of central Indiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fatal car accident occurred on I-74 between the State Road 9 and State Road 44 exits shortly after noon. A westbound Honda crossed over the median and collided with an eastbound Nissan. Three males in the Honda were injured, and one of them, who has not been identified, died at IU Health-Methodist Hospital in Indianapolis. Snow was falling at the time and was determined to possibly be the cause of the accident.

Numerous thunderstorms formed ahead of a cold front during the late afternoon, then continued into the evening. Some supercells and bowing line segments were embedded within the large area of convection through the evening hours. A significant tornado occurred in southwest Indiana, along with isolated damaging winds. The atmosphere was only marginally unstable, with surface-based capes around 1000. Strong wind shear was favorable for the organized storm structures, including a few supercells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado crossed the Ohio River from Henderson County, Kentucky into Newburgh. Five homes lost at least portions of their roof, and at least one home lost some of its second story walls. Dozens of other homes received moderate to major damage, including several with fallen trees causing major damage. Hundreds of trees were snapped or uprooted. Dozens of small buildings, garages, and other small structures were damaged. Many power lines were either blown down or brought down by fallen trees. At least two people were treated and released from area hospitals. The tornado was photographed near the beginning of its track along the Ohio River. Peak winds were estimated near 125 mph.

A third round during the late evening and overnight brought additional heavy rain and damaging wind gusts.  A microburst caused an intense area of significant damage to portions of Morgan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Relayed via the Skywarn network.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms formed ahead of a cold front during the late afternoon, then continued into the evening. Some supercells and bowing line segments were embedded within the large area of convection through the evening hours. A significant tornado occurred in southwest Indiana, along with isolated damaging winds. The atmosphere was only marginally unstable, with surface-based capes around 1000. Strong wind shear was favorable for the organized storm structures, including a few supercells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On March 19th and 20th, two cold fronts had an affect on the region. The front on the 19th caused heavy rain to drop into southern Indiana. The front on the 20th was a long front that stretched from the Great Lakes straight to the middle of Texas. As this front moved towards the southeast, it caused a line of severe winds and localized flooding over much of the southeastern United States.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a car stuck in high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong clipper-like system moved towards the region from the Northern Plains. A cold front pushed from west to east across the area starting around 15z and exited the area around 20z. Temperatures started the day in the upper 40s to low 50s and rose to the upper 50s to around 60 degrees. Dew points started in the low 30s and rose into the mid 40s out ahead of the line. A 30 to 40 kt low level jet traversed the southern periphery of Lake Michigan as the low passed by to our north. Steep mid level lapse rates approaching 8 C/km were seen due to unseasonably cold air aloft (500 mb temps -25 to -27 C). These factors lead to low topped thunderstorms which produced sporadic wind damage. A 77 mph wind gust was recorded at the Fort Wayne Regional Airport as a cell collapsed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management officials reported two power poles were snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours of May 23rd, thunderstorms moved across portions of central Indiana.  Most of the storms remained subsevere, but a few stronger storms brought periods of large hail and damaging winds to the area.

Minor flooding of the Ohio River occurred during early May in response to a few rounds of thunderstorms from late April into early May. The flooding in May was minor. Low-lying fields, a few river access roads, and some riverfront parkland was under water. Some crop damage occurred to early planted crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was located across northern Indiana, helping to enhance effective shear (40 to 50 kts). Over 1000 J/KG of MLCAPE was in place ahead of 2 HP supercells that developed across portions of northern Illinois into northwestern Indiana. The first storm tracked from neat Joliet, IL and then turned east, moving into LaPorte and St Joseph counties before dissipating. Immediately behind this storm, another supercell developed in Will County, Indiana and moved east from southern LaPorte county into the Plymouth area before quickly dissipating. DCAPE over 1000 J/KG aided in damaging wind development, with at least 1 rotating wall cloud noted west of Plymouth. No tornadoes were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread frost occurred over southwest Indiana. A strong high pressure system was centered over the Ozark Mountains early on the 9th, resulting in clear skies and chilly northwest winds. During the overnight hours of May 8th going into May 9th, a widespread hard freeze occurred across much of Indiana.  Temperatures ranged from the low 30s in southern Indiana to the mid 20s in central Indiana and low 20s in northern Indiana.  This freeze caused widespread damage to fruit and fruit trees from central Indiana and northern Indiana with lesser impacts in southern Indiana.  Row crops like corn and soybeans were damaged and some needed to be replanted in north central Indiana with soybeans being the most impacted. At this time, there remains to be significant uncertainty on the extent of the damage and what the financial costs will end up being.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An official with Purdue Agriculutural extension in Tipton county reported damage to row crops of soybean and corn in Tipton county.  Impacts to corn were relatively minor with more serious effects to soybeans.  Some needed to be replanted.  Strawberries and fruit trees were hit hard, but monetary amounts of damage are still uncertain at this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe weather outbreak occurred during the evening hours of April 8th. A line of severe thunderstorms moved through central Indiana intitially causing large hail and damaging winds. As the storms progressed, the hail threat gradually decreased with wind speeds rapidly increasing. In addition to widespread 60 to 70 mph winds, the line of storms had several mesovortices that led to areas of enhanced winds up to 90 mph and produced an EF-1 tornado in Mooresville, IN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Part of roof blown off of house due to thunderstorm wind gusts near County Road 850 N and South Paddock Road.

During the evening hours of June 3rd a series of thunderstorms brought pockets of severe weather to portions of central Indiana.  A few thunderstorms became severe north of the Indianapolis metro which led to thunderstorm wind damage.  A microburst from another thunderstorm caused fairly significant damage in Rush County near the county seat of Rushville.  Thunderstorms continued into the night with flash flooding warnings issued.  No reports of flash flooding were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees reported to be downed due to thunderstorm wind gusts and were blocking a nearby road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong upper level low pressure system and surface cold front moved through central Indiana during the late morning and afternoon hours of June 10th.  A very humid air mass was in place behind a tropical system that had passed through.  This combined with the strong dynamics allowed for storms with very strong wind gusts and some large hail to form.  The strongest storms were north and east of the forecast area. Numerous reports of winds up to 70 mph and hail as large as an inch occurred with this storm system.  The most severe damage occurred in portions of Tipton and Howard counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail cracked wind shield.

Isolated wind damage was noted in Elkhart county along with dime size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under a narrow ridge of high pressure, clear skies and light winds were conducive for widespread freezing temperatures. Observed low temperatures on the morning of the 15th ranged from the upper 20's to 30 degrees. The low temperature of 29 degrees at Evansville was a record low for the date. Fruit crops were damaged. The degree of damage was dependent on the site, crop, and variety of fruit. In particular, apples, peaches, and grapes were vulnerable to a freeze. The asparagus harvest was stopped by the freeze. Freeze-sensitive perennials in gardens were green and actively growing when the freeze occurred.

Temepratures across Indiana fell into the mid to upper 20s during the overnight hours of April 15th into the 16th.  This cold weather led to significant crop damage across central and southern Indiana. Much of northern Indiana was not impacted as significantly due to the difference in crop type and that it was still early in the season.  The value of damage is unknown at this time, but reports show that a significant portion of some of the crops in southern Indiana may be affected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A freeze brought damage to crops across Indiana. Individual reports in Indiana vegetables indicate some damage in vegetables across the area.Asparagus harvest in the south had begun but stopped due to frost/freeze. Some cool season crops were killed/damaged in north central Indiana (peas, onions). Some tomatoes transplanted into high tunnels were damaged/killed in central Indiana. Apples, peaches and grapes were at stages sensitive to damage. It���s still a little early to know the full effect on crop yield, but it appears that in some cases, there will be little crop while in others fruit thinning will still be necessary.

An unusually strong upper level low pressure system and surface cold front moved through central Indiana during the late morning and afternoon hours of June 10th.  A very humid air mass was in place behind a tropical system that had passed through.  This combined with the strong dynamics allowed for storms with very strong wind gusts and some large hail to form.  The strongest storms were north and east of the forecast area. Numerous reports of winds up to 70 mph and hail as large as an inch occurred with this storm system.  The most severe damage occurred in portions of Tipton and Howard counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple tree limbs and various backyard items blown away by thunderstorm wind gusts. Live power line was wrapped around a trampoline. Tree was also reported on fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the overnight of January 17th into the morning of the 18th, a wintery mix of freezing rain and some snow fell north of the I-70 corridor as it moved east across the region. A relatively warm ground and warming air temperature negated most of the adverse effects fairly quickly in the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tenth of an inch of ice was measured in Centerville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another multicell day with short-lived but intense cores due to moderate instability/low shear. Outflow interactions dominated the evolution once again. Storms seemed to focus south of Route 6, possibly due to effects from outflow boundary that settled across the area the night before. Not a great deal of severe weather but FWA airport reported a 67 mph wind gust. A few reports of half inch to penny sized hail across the southeast along with some flooding in the Fort Wayne area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Also estimated 50 mph winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on County Road 500 W from Cornell Ditch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The river flood season got off to an early start as the year 2020 opened. Rather mild and wet conditions continued into the new year. Rainfall totals across the region were 1 to over 3 inches above normal for January. The subtropical jetstream was very active, drawing in moisture from the eastern Pacific and the Gulf of Mexico. This provided very heavy rainfall, especially during the first half of the month. Heavy rainfall on the 11th caused many of the region's rivers to rise rapidly. The mainstem White and Wabash Rivers were already high and just began rising again.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred on the White River. The river crested at 25.53 feet on the 19th at the Hazleton river gage. Moderate flooding begins at 24 feet. People in residential cabins on the riverward side of the levee generally relocate at this level. Many local roads were completely impassable. Oil fields were inaccessible. Water began to come onto the street at the corner of West 2nd Street and Brown Street in the town of Hazleton. Extensive flooding of agricultural fields occurred. Since this flood occurred primarily outside of the growing season, little if any crop damage occurred.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred on the Wabash River. The river crested at 27.85 feet on the 20th at the Mt. Carmel river gage. Moderate flooding begins at 25 feet. Cattle and farm equipment were relocated. Extensive lowland flooding occurred. Flood waters began to affect residents of East Mt. Carmel. Fifth Street that parallels the river was closed. A  restaurant along the river closed. Several local river roads flooded. The Wabash River was more than 2 miles wide at I-64.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The river flood season got off to an early start as the year 2020 opened. Rather mild and wet conditions continued into the new year. Rainfall totals across the region were 1 to over 3 inches above normal for January. The subtropical jetstream was very active, drawing in moisture from the eastern Pacific and the Gulf of Mexico. This provided very heavy rainfall, especially during the first half of the month. Heavy rainfall on the 11th caused many of the region's rivers to rise rapidly. The mainstem White and Wabash Rivers were already high and just began rising again.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred on the White River. The river crested at 24.76 feet on the 18th at the Petersburg river gage. Moderate flooding begins at 23.5 feet. Extensive bottomland flooding affected agricultural and rural residential areas. Several rural county roads flooded. A few residents moved out of an area locally known as Dodge City. Several small oil fields were inaccessible. Since this occurred primarily outside of agricultural season, little or no crop damage occurred. High water isolated some river cabin residents. State Road 257 was completely flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred on the Wabash River. The river crested at 20.17 feet on the 21st at the New Harmony, IN river gage. Moderate flooding begins at 20.0 feet. Considerable flooding of low ground occurred, but most of the low ground was dormant agricultural fields. Some county roads were flooded. Water encroached on parts of northern New Harmony, and Harmonie State Park was closed. Some stripper oil wells were flooded along the river from New Harmony to Mt. Vernon Indiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stretching from northern Indiana south along the Mississippi River Valley moved east through southern Indiana and central Kentucky. This powerful storm system brought widespread and locally heavy rain to the area, along with damaging gradient and thunderstorm winds throughout the day. High temperatures dropped 20 degrees from January 11 to 12. Widespread 40-60 MPH winds occurred across the region in and out of storms, and through several waves during the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down on various county roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northeast winds gusting as high as 50 mph produced waves as high as 18 feet along the Lake Michigan shore of northwest Indiana.  These high waves combined with high lake levels produced shoreline flooding, erosion and damage.  In Lake County, Lakefront Park in Whiting Indiana was flooded. In Porter County, beach erosion and flooding occurred at Portage Lakefront and Riverwalk Pavilion.

An unseasonably strong storm system brought heavy rainfall and isolated flooding on January 10-11th. The bulk of the event occurred overnight and into the morning hours on the 11th. Rainfall totals ranged from 1 to 3 inches, which resulted in isolated flash flooding. Strong forcing associated with a potent 500 mb shortwave tapped into a very moist southwest wind flow. A weak surface low formed along a cold front over southwest Arkansas, then deepened as it tracked northeast across the Missouri Bootheel and into the lower Ohio Valley on the morning of the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 1.2 inches of rain in the past hour caused flooding a couple miles southeast of Evansville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low pressure system developed ahead of an upper level trough of low pressure. As a cold front crossed, strong winds created scattered damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Received as a CWOP report from Vevay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the overnight of January 17th into the morning of the 18th, a wintery mix of freezing rain and some snow fell north of the I-70 corridor as it moved east across the region. A relatively warm ground and warming air temperature negated most of the adverse effects fairly quickly in the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tenth of an inch of ice was measured in Connersville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved from the Midwest into the lower Ohio Valley on April 8, 2020. Isolated storms developed ahead of a sinking cold front during the afternoon across portions of southern Indiana and central Kentucky. These storms dropped large hail and produced damaging winds. Later that evening, a line of severe thunderstorms moved in from the northwest and produced widespread damaging winds and small hail. Storms pushed east of the region during the early morning hours of April 9, 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Storm Survey confirmed 90 mph straight-line wind damage near Hibernia in Clark County, IN.  The strong winds removed half the roof of a newer large barn/home in addition to damaging multiple trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe weather outbreak occurred during the evening hours of April 8th. A line of severe thunderstorms moved through central Indiana intitially causing large hail and damaging winds. As the storms progressed, the hail threat gradually decreased with wind speeds rapidly increasing. In addition to widespread 60 to 70 mph winds, the line of storms had several mesovortices that led to areas of enhanced winds up to 90 mph and produced an EF-1 tornado in Mooresville, IN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow plows were needed to clear the volume of tree damage and debris from roads across town.  Numerous tree and tree limbs snapped due to thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnant circulation from tropical storm Cristobal combined with strong, negatively tilted phasing shortwave disturbance moved across the area providing strong synoptic winds ahead of the storms. Strong, dynamic cold front initiated storms just after the noon hour with damaging winds, hail, and heavy rain observed during the afternoon. Strong post frontal drying within fierce mid level dry intrusion yielded 40 to 60 mph wind gusts and blowing dust late afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EM reports numerous trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A training thunderstorm complex during the night of June 27th into the 28th produced significant flash flooding.  A weak mid-level disturbance advanced east early in the morning across eastern IL. This energy helped foster thunderstorm activity across portions of southern IL, southern IN, and Kentucky. The air mass around the southern flank of the disturbance was still at least modestly unstable with mixed layer capes around 1000 j/kg early in the morning. A west-southwest low-level wind flow between 30 and 40 knots focused strong moisture transport into the storms. The storms trained over the same area for three to five hours. Precipitable water values across the region were on the order of 1.7 to 1.9 inches, fueling rainfall rates exceeding 1 inch per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some roads were covered with water. An observer at the Evansville museum measured 3.80 inches within 20 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southern Indiana sat to the east of a stacked low that was positioned over eastern Kansas and northwestern Missouri. A warm front stretched east form this low pressure system to the Mid-Atlantic. As this front moved northward through Indiana to usher in warmer temperatures and saturated air, it brought heavy rains and thunderstorms to Perry County, Indiana which resulted in areal flooding and isolated wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Starlight Road, near Tell City, was down to one lane due to a partial culvert washout.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved from the Midwest into the lower Ohio Valley on April 8, 2020. Isolated storms developed ahead of a sinking cold front during the afternoon across portions of southern Indiana and central Kentucky. These storms dropped large hail and produced damaging winds. Later that evening, a line of severe thunderstorms moved in from the northwest and produced widespread damaging winds and small hail. Storms pushed east of the region during the early morning hours of April 9, 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were downed on 36th Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: May 23, warmer temperatures surged into central Kentucky and southern Indiana behind a northward moving warm front. The surge of warm moist air resulted in scattered thunderstorms producing wind damage that occurred for three days from Logan County, Kentucky to southern Indiana before coming to an end in the Bluegrass region of Kentucky. Kentucky resulted in more storm reports from this event over the three day period, but southern Indiana saw reports of wind damage and flooding on the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on Hickory Grove Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnant circulation from tropical storm Cristobal combined with strong, negatively tilted phasing shortwave disturbance moved across the area providing strong synoptic winds ahead of the storms. Strong, dynamic cold front initiated storms just after the noon hour with damaging winds, hail, and heavy rain observed during the afternoon. Strong post frontal drying within fierce mid level dry intrusion yielded 40 to 60 mph wind gusts and blowing dust late afternoon and evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level disturbance produced a long duration snow event late February 25th through early February 27th. Snowfall totals ranged between 3 and 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of light to occasionally moderate snow and below freezing temperatures created difficult travel conditions February 26th through early February 27th. Snowfall totals across the county generally ranged between 3 and 4 inches. Untreated and lesser traveled roads became snow covered and slick in spots, especially during the overnight and morning hours. Numerous school cancellations and slide-offs were reported across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of Cristobal approaching from the west with a good dry slot developing toward Illinois/Indiana through the afternoon. With a very moist air mass (low 70s F surface dewpoints) also developing northward, dry slot-related cloud breaks and diurnal heating will contribute to weakening inhibition and moderate buoyancy by afternoon, particularly across Illinois into western/central Indiana. There was an SPC slight risk for severe storms with damaging wind and a few tornadoes possible.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EM reported two trees blown over, one onto a vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter weather system impacted much of central Indiana beginning Wednesday|February 12th, and lasting through 14th. Precipitation fell as snow in northern Indiana, with rain/freezing rain/snow in central Indiana, and rain southern Indiana. This led to slick road conditions during the Wednesday evening commute in both northern and central Indiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rollover crash with non-life threatening injuries occurred during the evening of February 12th. This accident occurred on Kessler Boulevard near Meridian Street.  Over 30 other slide offs and non-injuring accidents occurred during the evening and overnight hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnant circulation from tropical storm Cristobal combined with strong, negatively tilted phasing shortwave disturbance moved across the area providing strong synoptic winds ahead of the storms. Strong, dynamic cold front initiated storms just after the noon hour with damaging winds, hail, and heavy rain observed during the afternoon. Strong post frontal drying within fierce mid level dry intrusion yielded 40 to 60 mph wind gusts and blowing dust late afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EM reports tree in roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep 500 mb trough over the southwestern states lifted northeast across the central states. A surface low passed east across the lower Mississippi Valley, producing a swath of heavy rain across the lower Ohio Valley. A plume of very moist air aloft accompanied the upper-level trough as it lifted northeast. Although rainfall amounts were not especially heavy, they fell on very moist ground that was primed for flooding issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 1.4 inches of rain over the past 12 hours, with flash flooding in the Angel Mounds community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred on the Wabash, White, and Ohio Rivers. Low-lying woods and fields in the floodplains were inundated. Several low-lying county roads were closed along the White River in Gibson and Pike Counties. Near the Wabash River, all oil field production ceased with the exception of pumping units on substructures. On the Ohio River, some county roads in the bottomlands were flooded and closed. Ohio River floodwaters backed into Pigeon Creek near Evansville. Near Newburgh on the Ohio River, Highway 662 near the old Lock and Dam 47 began to flood. A wet February contributed to another period of river flooding. Monthly rainfall was between 100 and 125 percent of normal.

During the afternoon and evening hours of July 11th, a series of thunderstorms moved across central and north central Indiana producing widespread damaging winds and large hail.  Significant power outages occurred across Indianapolis and surrounding cities.  During the overnight hours, a more robust line of thunderstorms brought additional wind damage and large hail to areas that had already experienced thunderstorms earlier in the day.||Many areas had several rounds of thunderstorms.  Times were not always exact and could have been from one of several thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees downed due to thunderstorm wind gusts across the southern portions of Parke County with the majority near Rosedale.

During the afternoon and evening hours of July 27th, a line of thunderstorms moved across central Indiana causing sporadic wind damage.  The thunderstorms initially began producing damaging winds just west of Indianapolis where a wind gust knocked down a partially constructed building causing 3 injuries and 1 fatality. Further east, tree damage occurred from Indianapolis to Rushville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power pole was knocked down and laying over the line near the intersection of County Roads 700N and 800E.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late evening hours of July 31st, a nearly stationary storm formed over portions of Martin and Daviess county.  Over the course of 3 hours, 6 to 8 inches of rain fell across areas that had received 4 to 6 inches the day before.  This led to significant flooding near the town of Shoals as well as other rural portions of Martin and Daviess counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 450 was closed for most of the night due to up to 3 feet of water that was flowing over the road.  Highway 50 was also closed along with numerous roads within the town of Shoals.  Flooding was noted to be the most severe in recent memory.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold front sagging south into very unstable air mass with deep layer shear 25-30 kts. There was an SPC slight risk for severe weather with hail and wind being primary threats. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few quarter sized hailstones, though most were penny or smaller. Reported via twitter.

A line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved into southwest Indiana during the late evening. A 500 mb disturbance over northern Missouri moved east into west central Illinois during the evening. An outflow boundary from morning storms was the focus for thunderstorm development in east central Missouri. These storms propagated east-southeast across southern Illinois into southwest Indiana by late in the evening, fed by a very warm and moist southwest wind flow. Moderately strong winds aloft associated with the upper level disturbance contributed to an isolated tornadic storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado damage was mainly to trees and power lines. A couple houses lost some shingles and fascia. At least one vehicle was damaged by a fallen tree. Corn was blown down in a strongly convergent pattern. There was a strong cross-wind component to the snapped and uprooted trees. Estimated maximum wind speeds were near 95 mph. Survey findings were compiled from drone footage and data provided by the Evansville-Vanderburgh and Posey County emergency management agencies in conjunction with NWS data. The tornado path continued into Vanderburgh County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms occurred in the heat of the afternoon north of a weak surface boundary extending from western Tennessee to South Carolina. One of the storms produced a lightning strike that killed a construction worker in Evansville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A construction worker on the roof of a building was struck by lightning. The individual was a 34-year-old male who was on top of a tall building in downtown Evansville. The lightning hit the roof, causing damage about the size of a softball. The individual was near the damage site when he was struck. He received a burn mark on his chest but no other obvious injuries. The man was admitted to a hospital and then passed away two days later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening hours of July 27th, a line of thunderstorms moved across central Indiana causing sporadic wind damage.  The thunderstorms initially began producing damaging winds just west of Indianapolis where a wind gust knocked down a partially constructed building causing 3 injuries and 1 fatality. Further east, tree damage occurred from Indianapolis to Rushville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A partially constructed building collapsed due to non-severe thunderstorm wind gusts associated with a gust front ahead of an approaching line of thunderstorms.  At the time, only the wooden framing was in place which allowed for the collapse to occur more easily when the wind gusts arrived.  No other damage was noted in the immediate area and only very small limbs (inch or less in diameter) were down in the area indicating wind gusts were less than 50 knots. 3 people that were working on the building at the time were injured with a fatality also occurring at the location where the wood frame collapsed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into the area with widely scattered thunderstorms along it. Marginal instability existed to allow for several stronger storms. One did produce locally damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management officials reported a tree limb down onto a power line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The initial gust front, associated with the derecho noted in a separate episode, passed over the Michigan City nearshore buoy (45170) around 4:40 pm CDT, with one foot waves being reported prior to the arrival of the storm. By 5:00 pm CDT, waves were reported at three feet, and by 6:30 pm CDT the waves peaked at six feet. Wave action continued into the early morning hours of the 11th. No reports of flooding were received, however, near record high water levels existed with the overall angle of the winds compared to the shoreline orientation limiting flooding impacts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of an upper trough, warm moist air stretched towards northeastern Indiana between a surface low and high near the Lower Ohio Valley. As the atmosphere became more unstable with diurnal heating, thunderstorms developed.  A tree was reported down from the outflow of one of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported tree damage on State Road 111 near Rosewood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated slow-moving thunderstorm produced very heavy rain. The storm occurred ahead of a cold front as it moved southeast through a very moist air mass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor street flooding was reported in Newburgh after 1.27 inches fell in 35 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent surface low lifted across the western Great Lakes on November 15, 2020, bringing two rounds of convection and high winds. Wind gusts of 50 to 65 mph caused power outages, structural damage, and blew over trees across northeast Indiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 60 mph wind gust was recorded just south of the Grissom Air Reserve Base. Numerous reports of tree limbs being blown down were received as well as power line or power pole damage (mainly from falling tree limbs).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow, yet intense, lake effect snow band set up over central and eastern portions of La Porte County on November 30th. Slushy accumulations of 2 to 5 inches created difficult travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy lake effect snow created difficult travel on November 30th, especially during the late morning and afternoon hours in central and eastern portions of La Porte County. Slushy accumulations of 2 to 5 inches were common across the area, with visibilities reduced to a quarter of a mile at times. There was an incident on the Indiana Toll Road near the St. Joseph county border that closed down westbound lanes for several hours for a jackknifed semi-truck. There were no injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent surface low lifted across the western Great Lakes on November 15, 2020, bringing two rounds of convection and high winds. Wind gusts of 50 to 65 mph caused power outages, structural damage, and blew over trees across northeast Indiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fort Wayne International Airport recorded a peak wind gust of 63 mph, with several others in the 55 to 60 mph range. Numerous reports of tree limbs being blown down were received as well as power line or power pole damage (mainly from falling tree limbs).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak cold front approached the region with very warm and moist airmass ahead of it. Broken line of storm clusters impacted mainly our southern and eastern areas with damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several four to five inch diameters trees downed, in good health.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak cold front approached the region with very warm and moist airmass ahead of it. Broken line of storm clusters impacted mainly our southern and eastern areas with damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two tree limbs downed, each around six inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms developed once again across the area. While shear remained too weak for organization, sufficient instability was in place for a few storms to pulse to strong or marginally severe. The primary hazard with storms was locally heavy rain, torrential at times, and strong wind gusts. There were also isolated reports of hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EM reported semi truck and exit sign blown over. Time estimated from radar.

A weak landspout tornado occurred in association with a light rain shower. Isolated convection formed under a weak trough in the mid levels of the atmosphere extending from the central Great Lakes to the Tennessee Valley. Low level lapse rates were steep, averaging near 8 degrees Celsius per kilometer in the lowest 3 km.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landspout tornado was documented, with pictures and video posted on social media. The narrow rope-like funnel was associated with a cumulus cloud containing little if any precipitation. The location was approximated by the observer about one mile south of Highway 356 near County Road 300 E. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Lower Ohio Valley sat under the front of an upper ridge as a surface low advanced towards the state. This low, anchoring a warm front to the east and cold front to the west, caused thunderstorms to breakout across southern Indiana as the system moved through the area. The storms produced wind and hail damage across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down along State Road 56 near Salem.

Broken lines of thunderstorms moved southeast across southwest Indiana during the heat of the afternoon. The storms occurred ahead of a cold front that extended from central Illinois to west central Missouri. Precipitable water values ranged from 1.5 to 1.9 inches, which resulted in locally heavy downpours and isolated flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over County Road 550 S between State Routes 64 and 168.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With zonal flow over the country and high temperatures in the 90s, multiple weak surface fronts help to produce afternoon and evening storms during diurnal heating. These storms produced strong winds, lightning, and heavy rainfall that resulted in wind damage,  flooding, and a structure fire from lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Road 60 at Deam Lake was impassible due to flooding. This is a typical flood spot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening hours of July 11th, a series of thunderstorms moved across central and north central Indiana producing widespread damaging winds and large hail.  Significant power outages occurred across Indianapolis and surrounding cities.  During the overnight hours, a more robust line of thunderstorms brought additional wind damage and large hail to areas that had already experienced thunderstorms earlier in the day.||Many areas had several rounds of thunderstorms. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs, trees, and power lines downed due to thunderstorm wind gusts in the nearby area. A roof was blown off a house near the intersection of State Road 39 and County Road 700N.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced very heavy rain across the lower Ohio Valley. The storms occurred in a moist and unstable air mass ahead of a slow-moving 500 mb disturbance over south central Illinois. The slow-moving storms initiated along a weak|low level convergence axis stretching from east central Missouri into southern Illinois and northwestern Kentucky. Precipitable water values across the region ranged from 1.6 to 2.0 inches, supporting locally heavy rainfall rates near two inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was observed on the west side of Newburgh. A rainfall amount of 2.82 inches was reported near Deaconess Gateway Hospital. The rainfall total was for the afternoon, and the majority fell within one hour.

During the late evening hours of July 31st, a nearly stationary storm formed over portions of Martin and Daviess county.  Over the course of 3 hours, 6 to 8 inches of rain fell across areas that had received 4 to 6 inches the day before.  This led to significant flooding near the town of Shoals as well as other rural portions of Martin and Daviess counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 450 was closed for most of the night due to up to 3 feet of water that was flowing over the road.  Highway 50 was also closed along with numerous roads within the town of Shoals.  Flooding was noted to be the most severe in recent memory. The exact end time for when the shift from Flash Flood to flood is uncertain with an estimate used for the purposes of Storm Data.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado first touched down along Milton Bear Branch Road in western Ohio County. The tornado traveled east southeast until it lifted near the 6000 block of Palmer Road. There were numerous occurrences of damage to roofs, siding, barns and trees. Damage of this nature was consistent with an EF0 tornado, with winds topping out at about 70 to 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A group of 5 teens were swimming in Lake Michigan near Portage Riverwalk when waves pulled them under.  A good Samaritan riding a jet ski helped pull two swimmers out.  Two others managed to swim out of the currents.  One of the swimmers pulled out of the water, an 18 year old male, was taken to a local hospital and died a few days later.  The body of the 5th and missing swimmer, a 16 year old boy, was found the next day.  Waves were around 4 feet.||In a separate incident, a 21 year old male was rescued off Portage Beach after being underwater for an extended period.  He had gone missing while swimming in Lake Michigan.  He was taken to a local hospital where he died a few hours later.  Rescuers received many calls for swimmers struggling in Lake Michigan on August 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance approaching the lower Ohio Valley triggered a large thunderstorm complex that produced heavy rainfall. Although it was small-scale, the slow movement of this disturbance produced torrential rainfall of 1 to 3 inches over a few hours. Mean winds through the depth of the atmosphere were generally 5 knots or less to the north-northwest. Precipitable water values were above two inches, which is near the daily record.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over the roads in many locations across the county, however they were all passable.

During the evening hours of November 25th, a fast moving upper level system and surface cold front brought widespread rain and scattered thunderstorms to much of central Indiana.  Storms remained below severe criteria for much of the day until a portion of the line began to bulge out in southeastern Indiana.  This bulge briefly began to show signs of rotation and produced damaging thunderstorm wind gusts across portions of Shelby and Bartholomew counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree of unknown size was downed by thunderstorm wind gusts along the 1200 block of County Road 1100 South.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked northeast along the Appalachians. Rain over the Ohio Valley mixed with and changed to snow from west to east during the day of Monday, November 30th. Snowfall accumulations continued into the early morning hours of Tuesday, December 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer north of Batesville measured 1.2 inches of snow. Other observers near Napoleon and northeast of Osgood measured an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On August 10th, an intense derecho brought severe to extreme winds to much of the Midwest with winds estimated as high as 130 to 140 mph in Iowa.  As the derecho neared Indiana, the storms were not quite as intense, but did produce measured winds up to 70 mph and caused significant tree damage and power outages to the local area. Over 100,000 people were without power at one point with power outages lasting several days across portions of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree of unknown size was blown down into a house near Anderson University by  thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late morning through the afternoon of Monday August 10th, a line of intense thunderstorms known as a derecho brought widespread severe wind damage across Iowa, northern Illinois and northern Indiana.||In northwest Indiana alone, the winds downed hundreds of trees, as well as caused damage to homes, businesses and infrastructure.  There were two injuries reported in northwest Indiana within the NWS Chicago/Romeoville County Warning Area (CWA) due to falling trees. It was estimated that over 200,000 customers lost power at the height of the derecho across all of northern Indiana.  While much of the damage was due to straight line winds, there was also one tornado documented in northwest Indiana.||The combination of high moisture, explosive instability and very strong atmospheric wind flow for mid-summer had developed quickly over the Corn Belt region on the morning of August 10th.  This resulted in a progressive, well-organized complex of storms for hundreds of miles and at times traveling nearly 70 mph.  After bringing extensive damage from extreme wind speeds in Iowa that morning, the damage transitioned across northern Illinois from widespread wind damage to narrow, brief and intense swaths associated with compact circulations within the thunderstorm line, some of which were associated with tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were snapped in St. John.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late morning through the afternoon of Monday August 10th, a line of intense thunderstorms known as a derecho brought widespread severe wind damage across Iowa, northern Illinois and northern Indiana.||In northwest Indiana alone, the winds downed hundreds of trees, as well as caused damage to homes, businesses and infrastructure. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked northeast along the Appalachians. Rain over the Ohio Valley mixed with and changed to snow from west to east during the day of Monday, November 30th. Snowfall accumulations continued into the early morning hours of Tuesday, December 1st.

As the derecho neared Indiana, the storms were not quite as intense, but did produce measured winds up to 70 mph and caused significant tree damage and power outages to the local area. Over 100,000 people were without power at one point with power outages lasting several days across portions of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree roughly 18 inches in diameter was snapped due to thunderstorm wind gusts and fell onto utility lines.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An inch and a half of snow was measured in Liberty.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early on September 3rd, a surface low embedded along a cold front moved across Southern Indiana. This brought areas of heavy rain and cooler temperatures to the Lower Ohio Valley. While most of the issues with flooding occurred in Kentucky, isolated flooding observed near Tell City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over State Road 37 just north of Tell City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary, as well as several outflow boundaries, interacted with a weakly unstable environment to produce scattered showers and thunderstorms. A series of mergers allowed one thunderstorm in Huntington county to produce a handful of reports of damaging wind as well as marginally several hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size fell for 5 minutes at the Huntington North High School.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated strong storm produced large hail across Tippecanoe county during the evening hours of September 8th.  A frontal boundary was located north of the forecast area and a thunderstorm formed as a result of afternoon heating.  This storm rapidly grew in height before briefly producing hail up to golf balls. The night before, a complex of storms moved over north central Indiana which brought heavy rain to portions of Tipton and Howard counties.  This rain brought flooding to the town of Windfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mostly nickel sized but a few were up to the size of golf balls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northerly winds gusting as high as 45 mph produced waves as high as 18 feet along the Lake Michigan shore of northwest Indiana.  These high waves combined with high lake levels to produce shoreline flooding.  In Lake County, Lakefront Park in Whiting was flooded.

A wildfire started south of Interstate 80/94 near the north Little Calumet River west of Chase Street.  The fire spread north toward the interstate just west of Chase Street and crossed over Interstate 80/94 with traffic impacted by thick smoke.  Multiple fire response agencies were involved with fighting the fire, including a Lake County Sheriff's Department helicopter, which carried water in a bucket from a nearby canal.  Damage to utility poles occurred along the interstate.  North of the interstate, multiple parked vehicles and a shed were burned.  South winds were gusting as high as 30 mph with relative humidity levels around 30 percent.  The cause of the fire was not known.  No injuries or fatalities were reported.  The size of the fire was estimated around 50 acres, using high resolution satellite imagery.

A strong surface low in Wisconsin deepened and ejected northeast into Canada. A convective line with damaging thunderstorms occurred as a cold front crossed the region. Afterwards in the cold advection behind the front, damaging winds also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust to 50 mph was recorded in Batesville, while a gust of 46 mph was measured in Versailles. A newspaper reported fourteen hundred people were without power in Osgood due to power lines and poles that were downed by the strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low in Wisconsin deepened and ejected northeast into Canada. A convective line with damaging thunderstorms occurred as a cold front crossed the region. Afterwards in the cold advection behind the front, damaging winds also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fifteen hundred people were without power in Aurora due to power lines and poles that were downed by the strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening hours of November 25th, a fast moving upper level system and surface cold front brought widespread rain and scattered thunderstorms to much of central Indiana.  Storms remained below severe criteria for much of the day until a portion of the line began to bulge out in southeastern Indiana.  This bulge briefly began to show signs of rotation and produced damaging thunderstorm wind gusts across portions of Shelby and Bartholomew counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree of unknown size was downed by thunderstorm wind gusts at this location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds occurred ahead of a cold front as it sliced southeastward across the lower Ohio Valley. Gusts over 40 mph were recorded in parts of southwest Indiana. The highest reported measured gust was 43 mph at Evansville.

Another multicell day with short-lived but intense cores due to moderate instability/low shear. Outflow interactions dominated the evolution once again. Storms seemed to focus south of Route 6, possibly due to effects from outflow boundary that settled across the area the night before. Not a great deal of severe weather but FWA airport reported a 67 mph wind gust. A few reports of half inch to penny sized hail across the southeast along with some flooding in the Fort Wayne area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water reported over the road up to eight inches deep, on Covington Road between Jefferson Blvd. and Getz Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the overnight hours of November 14th into the 15th, a strong surface front approached central Indiana and passed through the state during the early morning hours.  Rain and isolated thunderstorms brought occasional wind gusts up to 45 mph with stronger winds occurring due to gradient winds behind the front during the mid morning hours.  The strongest wind gust was 64 mph with up to 50,000 people without power across the state at one point. Peak wind gusts were generally around 45 to 55 mph with isolated stronger gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 64 mph non-thunderstorm wind gust was measured at the KMIE ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of very weak flow aloft and an anomalously unstable and moist environment along a residual outflow boundary promoted the development of thunderstorms across the mid Mississippi and lower Ohio Valleys through the late afternoon into mid evening. A weak shortwave slowly advanced eastward along the bootheel of Missouri, while a weak impulse moved southeast from the northern border of Missouri. An outflow boundary was the primary focus for renewed development of thunderstorms in the afternoon. The environment was characterized by anomalously high precipitable water values, on the order of 1.8 to 2.1 inches, which is roughly 2 standard deviations above the climatological mean. A strong instability gradient existed as well, upwards of 5000 J/kg surface based cape across southern MO to northwest TN. A southwesterly inflow of 20-30 kts at 850 mb impinged on the boundary, sustaining the high precipitable water values and maximizing the convergence. Rain rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One foot of water was over one intersection on Highway 62. Numerous county roads were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening hours of June 3rd a series of thunderstorms brought pockets of severe weather to portions of central Indiana.  A few thunderstorms became severe north of the Indianapolis metro which led to thunderstorm wind damage.  A microburst from another thunderstorm caused fairly significant damage in Rush County near the county seat of Rushville.  Thunderstorms continued into the night with flash flooding warnings issued.  No reports of flash flooding were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of water flowing over several roads in the area. The duration of the time was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong upper level low pressure system and surface cold front moved through central Indiana during the late morning and afternoon hours of June 10th.  A very humid air mass was in place behind a tropical system that had passed through.  This combined with the strong dynamics allowed for storms with very strong wind gusts and some large hail to form.  The strongest storms were north and east of the forecast area. Numerous reports of winds up to 70 mph and hail as large as an inch occurred with this storm system.  The most severe damage occurred in portions of Tipton and Howard counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree approximately 2 feet in diameter downed onto house due to strong gradient winds. Home was located in Mooresville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably deep upper-level system ejected east into the Ohio Valley interacting with a very warm and tropically moist air mass that had settled into the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was knocked down near the 700 block of 11th and 12th Streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably deep upper-level system ejected east into the Ohio Valley interacting with a very warm and tropically moist air mass that had settled into the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was knocked down near the intersection of Straightline Pike and  Farlow Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An 18 year old male swimmer went missing around 11 am CDT on June 23rd near the west end of the beach of the Indiana Dunes National Lakeshore.  He was located one and a half hours later and taken to a local hospital where he was pronounced dead.  Waves were estimated around 4 feet.
An unseasonably deep upper-level system ejected east into the Ohio Valley interacting with a very warm and tropically moist air mass that had settled into the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was knocked down onto a house in the 800 block of Elk Country Club Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening hours evolved into a more widespread area of heavy rain and thunderstorms by the early overnight hours.  A few areas of isolated wind damage occurred in Vigo and Tippecanoe counties with most storms staying sub-severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Window was blown in at IU Arnett Hospital.  Damage is suspected to be due to thunderstorm wind gusts, but could not get a definitive answer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of subsevere thunderstorms moved through east central Indiana during the early afternoon hours of June 21st.  Ahead of this line, a lone thunderstorm formed in northwestern Rush county and briefly produced damaging wind gusts before being absorbed by the main line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines and trees downed to thunderstorm wind damage. Location is approximate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of very weak flow aloft and an anomalously unstable and moist environment along a residual outflow boundary promoted the development of thunderstorms across the mid Mississippi and lower Ohio Valleys through the late afternoon into mid evening. A weak shortwave slowly advanced eastward along the bootheel of Missouri, while a weak impulse moved southeast from the northern border of Missouri. An outflow boundary was the primary focus for renewed development of thunderstorms in the afternoon. The environment was characterized by anomalously high precipitable water values, on the order of 1.8 to 2.1 inches, which is roughly 2 standard deviations above the climatological mean. A strong instability gradient existed as well, upwards of 5000 J/kg surface based cape across southern MO to northwest TN. A southwesterly inflow of 20-30 kts at 850 mb impinged on the boundary, sustaining the high precipitable water values and maximizing the convergence. Rain rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred on the Lloyd Expressway on the east side of Evansville. A water rescue was reported at an intersection on the northeast side of Evansville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of thunderstorms moved across portions of northwest Indiana during the early morning hours of June 27th.  Many areas received 1 to 2 inches of rain.  The highest totals were near Kentland.  Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of June 27th producing very heavy rain over these same areas, including Kentland, producing flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters up to 2 feet in depth were reported on roads in and south of Rensselaer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of thunderstorms moved across portions of northwest Indiana during the early morning hours of June 27th.  Many areas received 1 to 2 inches of rain.  The highest totals were near Kentland.  Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of June 27th producing very heavy rain over these same areas, including Kentland, producing flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was first reported along Route 41 south of Kentland.  The fire chief said the water rose three feet in just 20 minutes.  The fire department was then flooded and equipment needed to be relocated.  Several fire departments assisted Kentland and rescued or evacuated 60 to 80 people from water filled streets, stranded cars and flooded homes.  Some of the rescues included vehicles that were flooded on Route 41 and Route 24 as well as county roads.  A family of three, which included an infant, was rescued from 1700 South.  Rescues on Route 24 occurred from the Indiana/Illinois state line to between Kentland and Goodland.||Three bridges and several county roads were washed out.  Numerous roads were flooded.  A fire truck was lost due to flooding.  Many homes were damaged and several homes received a considerable amount of damage.  The flood waters were five feet high in some homes and the flood waters were six feet deep in places on the south side of Kentland.  The flood waters began receding early in the morning of June 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy lake effect snow developed late in the night of December 24th and continued into the morning of December 25th.  Total snow accumulations of 4 to 15 inches with the greatest amounts across northern/northeast La Porte County and northern St. Joseph County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy lake effect snow from late evening of December 24th through late morning December 25th. Heaviest snow was early morning on December 25th. Storm total snowfall of 4 to 9 inches. There was a report of 8 inches in New Carlisle, IN just east of the La Porte County line. Visbilities were reduced to less than one quarter of a mile at times and snow covered roads created hazardous travel conditions. Reports of slide offs and accidents across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved across northwest Indiana during the late afternoon of December 29th into the early morning of December 30th.  A period of heavy snow occurred in the late afternoon into the evening when 2 to 3 inches of snow fell.  The precipitation transitioned to light freezing rain early in the morning of the 30th with many areas receiving up to one tenth of an inch of ice.

A winter storm moved across northwest Indiana during the late afternoon of December 29th into the early morning of December 30th.  A period of heavy snow occurred in the late afternoon into the evening when 2 to 3 inches of snow fell.  The precipitation transitioned to light freezing rain early in the morning of the 30th with many areas receiving up to one tenth of an inch of ice.

Heavy lake effect snow developed late in the night of December 24th and continued into the morning of December 25th.  Total snow accumulations of 4 to 15 inches with the greatest amounts across northern/northeast La Porte County and northern St. Joseph County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy lake effect snow from evening of December 24th through early afternoon December 25th. Heaviest snow was early morning on December 25th. Storm total snowfall of 6 to 15 inches. There was a report of 15 inches just northwest of South Bend. Visbilities were reduced to less than one quarter of a mile at times and snow covered roads created hazardous travel conditions. There were reports of slide offs and accidents across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure aloft combined with mid level energy to bring a shot of accumulating snowfall to parts of the Ohio Valley, especially along and east of the Scioto Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report from Hidden Valley indicated that 0.7 inches of snow accumulated there. The CoCoRaHS observers north of Bright and west of Lawrenceburg both measured a half inch of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure aloft combined with mid level energy to bring a shot of accumulating snowfall to parts of the Ohio Valley, especially along and east of the Scioto Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer southwest of Batesville measured 1.1 inches of snow. The cooperative observer southeast of Napoleon, and the EMA in Batesville both measured 0.3 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked northeast along the Appalachians. Rain over the Ohio Valley mixed with and changed to snow from west to east during the day of Monday, November 30th. Snowfall accumulations continued into the early morning hours of Tuesday, December 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An inch of snow was measured in Centerville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An inch and a half of snow was measured in Liberty.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked northeast along the Appalachians. Rain over the Ohio Valley mixed with and changed to snow from west to east during the day of Monday, November 30th. Snowfall accumulations continued into the early morning hours of Tuesday, December 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer north of Batesville measured 1.2 inches of snow. Other observers near Napoleon and northeast of Osgood measured an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked northeast along the Appalachians. Rain over the Ohio Valley mixed with and changed to snow from west to east during the day of Monday, November 30th. Snowfall accumulations continued into the early morning hours of Tuesday, December 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer west of Lawrenceburg measured 2.4 inches of snow, while the observer 4 miles west of Aurora measured 1.5 inches. Other observers near Dillsboro and Bright measured an inch of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level energy tracked northeast through the Ohio Valley ahead of an upper low pressure center tracking east through the Mississippi Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer southwest of Batesville measured 1.2 inches of snow, while another north of town measured an inch. A cooperative observer near Napoleon measured an inch of snow. Another northeast of Osgood measured 0.7 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level energy tracked northeast through the Ohio Valley ahead of an upper low pressure center tracking east through the Mississippi Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer 6 miles south-southeast of Moore's Hill measured 0.6 inches of snow. A half inch of snow was also measured east of Dillsboro, 4 miles west of Aurora, and north of Bright.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level energy tracked northeast through the Ohio Valley ahead of an upper low pressure center tracking east through the Mississippi Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer in Liberty measured 2 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convectively enhanced vorticity max lead to initiation of storms overnight Sunday across southern South Dakota. These initial storms grew upscale given concurrent downstream destabilization through northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa. Widespread 80-100 mph winds developed through central/eastern Iowa as this derecho matured. Northern bookend and advection wing forcing led to some supercell structures across northern Illinois/southwest Wisconsin. Northern bookend turned northeast as it moved across Lake Michigan and tracked into western Michigan. As this occurred, attendant post convective wake low weakened as it crossed the lake. Thus the local area did not experience the 80 to 100 mph winds that N IL/E IA experienced. However the resultant slowing and breakup of the line as it moved across northwest Indiana/SW Michigan, yielded a LEWP and subsequent multiple small scale circulations developing along the line. These meso-vortices were associated with pockets of enhanced wind damage and brief tornado occurrences.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a 25 foot tall tree was snapped two to three from the ground, landing onto a fence.

However the resultant slowing and breakup of the line as it moved across northwest Indiana/SW Michigan, yielded a LEWP and subsequent multiple small scale circulations developing along the line. These meso-vortices were associated with pockets of enhanced wind damage and brief tornado occurrences.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down by trained spotters.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado began west of the intersection of Riley Road and Dogwood Road in southeast St. Joseph County.  It then tracked along and south of Riley Road. Widespread tree and structural damage occurred through this path.  Grain bins were toppled and farm outbuildings sustained damage.  The tornado tracked through several cornfields that resulted in significant crop damage. The tornado lifted east of Beech Road. Maximum winds were estimated at 110 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary, combined with some tropical moisture from the remnants of Hurricane Lori to produce a broken line of pulse thunderstorms. A few reports of damaging wind occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a large tree was blown down across the road near the intersection of Mulberry and Madison Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were snapped west of State Route 13 as the tornado touched down. The tornado pushed over 2 power poles as it crossed SR 13, then continued southeast, causing a notable path of snapped trees and broken limbs. The debris was scattered throughout a nearby church parking lot. The church sustained minor loss of roof covering. Additional areas of tree damage were noted farther to the east-southeast. Some homes sustained damage as a result of falling trees and branches. The tornado crossed Webster Lake, coming onshore around EMS W17 Lane, where extensive tree damage occurred. Some residence damage was noted, both from the winds and falling trees. The tornado crossed East Backwater Road/East County Road 600 North and dissipated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down just west of the intersection of South County Road 700 North and South Beaver Dam Road, causing damage to trees to properties on either side of that intersection, with debris left on the roadway. A local fire chief witnessed the tornado lift over two structures and a grain bin with no noted damage. The tornado then touched down just east of the intersection in a mature corn field where it flattened a 100 to 150 foot circle in the corn, lifted briefly and then touched down again, causing the same type of damage before lifting again. The funnel attempted to touch down a third time but dissipated near a row of trees. Debris was still visible in the air. Maximum winds were estimated at 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong north winds gusting as high as 50 mph produced waves as high as 14 feet along the Lake Michigan shore of northwest Indiana.  These high waves combined with high lake levels produced shoreline flooding, erosion and damage.  In Lake County, Lakefront Park in Whiting, Indiana was completely inundated.  In Porter County, significant erosion caused damage to Lake Front Drive near Broadway Street in Beverly Shores.
